course_description:
An examination of some of the core concepts in political philosophy such as equality, community, liberty, ownership, state neutrality & autonomy. Different political philosophies & critical perspectives employing various interpretations of these concepts are also studied such as egalitarian, libertarian, feminist, & communitarian theories. Contemporary political issues are used to both illustrate the nature of the different theories & also as an aid in critically assessing them. This course may not run if there are fewer than 20 enrolments.

The course will examine some major moral and political philosophies along with introducingﾠthe method of Reflective Equilibrium as a methodology for thinking about moral and political philosophy.ﾠContemporary political and applied issues will be used to both illustrate the nature of the different philosophies & also as an aid in critically assessing them.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Students are to attend one 2 hour lecture and a 1 hour tutorial each week. There will be 10 tutorials in the semester.ﾠTutorials start in Week 2.ﾠ
course_aims: The main aims of this course is to guide students through a number of different political philosophies. In doing so, the focus will be on helping students develop conceptual frameworks through which they can understand and evaluate the arguments, policies, and political philosophies they encounter. They can then use these ideas to organise their intellectual and policy responses to the social world around them. Contemporary and applied issues will be used to both illustrate the nature of the different theories & also as an aid in critically assessing them.

learning_outcomes.description: identify and be able to characterize the arguments of some major political philosophers
critically evaluate and develop your own views about the different theories
characterize arguments for and against Rawls' principles of justice
characterize arguments for and against egalitarianism and welfarism
understand the notion of pareto optimality and its role in the discussion of distributive justice
characterize libertarianism and understand arguments for and against it as a political philosophy
characterize and be able to critique the arguments for and against intellectual property
characterize and be able to critique the arguments for and against the use of QALYs
understand reflective equilibrium and its role in the discussion of distributive justice
characterize and evaluate feminist political philosophy and critiques of liberalism

assessment_summary.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_summary.due_date: 14/04/2026 10:00 am; The exam will be a supervised exam during the lecture period.

assessment_details.title: Lecture and Tutorial Participation Activities
assessment_details.task_description: Participation in the lectures and tutorials includes polls, padlets; short, in-class quizzes; in-class reflections, discussions, and debates; and in-class steps working towards the development of your final essay. The participation assessment encourages students to attend regularly; engage in active Socratic thinking; stay current with lecture and reading content; and promote academic integrity in assessment. Its purposes also include helping students learn how to think philosophically (philosophy is not, like many disciplines, mainly about learning content but rather more about developing reasoning skills). Please see below for criteria associated with participation and quizzes. Guidance for preparation and meeting criteria for participation activities will be available on Blackboard. Allowance is built into the design of the participation mark for students to miss, without penalty, up to two of the scheduled lectures/tutorials for reasons not recognized by the university as acceptable exemptions from assessment (e.g. medical grounds or where there are exceptional circumstances - http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 ). You only need to contact the lecturer if you are absent from a class in cases where you will be applying for one of the University acceptable exemptions to assessment. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students are able to make-up participation activities where their absences meet university criteria such as medical grounds or exceptional circumstances. Contact the course coordinator for approval and make-up activities.
Allowance is built into the design of the participation mark for students to miss, without penalty, up to two of the scheduled lectures or tutorials for reasons not recognized by the university as acceptable exemptions from assessment (e.g. medical grounds or where there are exceptional circumstances - http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 ). You only need to contact the lecturer if you are absent from a class in cases where you will be applying for one of the University acceptable grounds for extension or make-up activities to assessment.

assessment_details.title: Final Essay
assessment_details.task_description: 1,800 word philosophy essay (+/-10%) Word count does not include titles or reference details in footnotes, in-text references, or bibliography; however, direct quotation and discursive footnotes are included in the word count. Topics will be made available via the Blackboard online teaching page. In line with the norm in Philosophy articles and books your essay should be written in the first person. In your essay you must critically engage with and evaluate arguments rather than just explain them and you must critically evaluate at least one objection to your argument. It is important at the beginning of your essay that you clearly set out the focus and scope of your argument. As you go along, it is often advantageous to flag some relevant arguments/claims that you are not going to address in detail. If the focus of the essay you are intending to write has some distance from the material we have covered in class then please consult the tutor or lecturer to get prior approval before starting to write your essay. There is an option to do the essay with one or two other students in the course. This is only an option - adopting this option is done solely via private voluntary arrangements between students. Group work is not a required part of the course and is not taught in this course. This option though gives students the opportunity to work collaboratively on their essays if they wish to. Students who choose to submit an essay jointly will receive an identical mark for the jointly submitted essay. The essay length will be 2100 words if two students are submitting, and 2400 words if three students are submitting. The name of the electronic file for your assignment and the title you submit through Blackboard should be the topic number and then your name (or names, if in a group), i.e. “Topic 2, Joe Blogs”. The actual wording of the question should not be typed on your assignment (these are simple requirements and if you do not comply with it you will lose a mark) Make sure you check, via your word processor program the word count of your paper to ensure it is within the required word limit (quotations and substantive footnotes count towards the word limit, bibliographies, in text references, titles or subtitles do not). Students need to provide their word count on their essay cover sheet. The assignments are designed so that you can demonstrate your understanding of the subject material, your analytical ability to reflect critically and evaluate the substantive arguments contained in the readings and lectures. A detailed guide for philosophy assignment preparation will be made available on the Blackboard site for this course. Students are responsible for familiarizing themselves with School policy regarding academic advice, assessment, plagiarism, etc. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Although students are permitted to use AI, they must complete an acknowledgement statement which will be available on the Blackboard online teaching page, and must comply with acknowledgement guidelines. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction Introduction to Issues in Political Philosophy and Applied Ethics and overview of what will be covered plus assessment and course expectations. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Welfarism and Utilitarianism The view that justice is about increasing people's welfare, with an in depth look at its common variant, utilitarianism. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Utilitarianism, QALYs Operationalizing Utilitarianism: QALYs. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L08
Rights and Utility; Reflective Equlibrium The relation of rights to utility; Reflective Equilibrium as a Methodology in political philosophy. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L09
Rawls' Political Philosophy The Political Philosophy of John Rawls Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Egalitarianism Egalitarianism; Equality and Pareto Optimality Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
In Semester Exam Exam to be held during lecture period Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L06, L10

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Week 9
learning_activities.topic: Liberalism, The Harm Principle, and Critiques Liberalism, The Harm Principle and Critiques from Feminist, Environmental, and Indigenous Perspectives Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L06

Libertarianism - Right and Associated Applied Issues Libertarian commitments to self-ownership, freedom, and property rights, with associated applied question such as past injustice and intellectual property. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06

Left Libertarianism & Consequent Applied Issues. Positions that combine libertarian commitments to self-ownership and freedom, with more egalitarian arguments about resources, as well as some of the arguments they raise about our attitudes to environmental resources and Indigenous connection to country. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06, L07

Conservativism Conservativism in Edmund Burke, Friedrich Hayek, and Michael Oakeshott, and its application to preservation of the environment, and cultural traditions. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
